lesson.name: Climate Adaptation and Disaster Response - Lesson 3: Pedagogies of Disaster

tasks.chapterTitle: A Pedagogy on the Verge of Disaster
Desocializing the School: Education and the Action-Zone
Art After Democratism and Concluding Papers

messages.content:
### Summary: Pedagogies of Disaster

This introductory section of the "Pedagogies of Disaster" conference proceedings explores the intersection of academic practice, institutional crisis, and the role of the "humanities" in a global context. 

The text is structured around the opening address by **Vincent W.J. van Gerven Oei** and a scholarly reflection by **Christopher Fynsk**.

#### **Key Points:**

*   **Institutional Constraints & Lobbying:** 
    *   Van Gerven Oei highlights the irony of hosting a conference on "disaster" while being bound by donor requirements (specifically from SOROS) that forbid "lobbying" or "calls to action." 
    *   He posits that pedagogical work—even when seemingly abstract or disconnected from immediate political goals—can become a "call to action" at a later, unforeseen time [S.30, S.34–S.35].

*   **Pedagogy as Dispossession:** 
    *   Christopher Fynsk analyzes Jean-François Lyotard’s essay "Endurance and the Profession," which describes the experience of teaching philosophy within an institution that has essentially collapsed (the radical freedom of the Vincennes department post-1968) [S.38].
    *   Fynsk identifies this as a "pedagogy of disaster," where the teacher lacks institutional protection or pre-given authority. In this state, the teacher must "give their name" to what they say, moving forward without knowing the destination [S.39–S.40].

*   **The "Local Intellectual" & The Humanities:** 
    *   The text explores the necessity of "bearing witness" to the bankruptcy of traditional academic institutions. 
    *   It argues for a practice of thought that eschews determinate, pre-packaged political ends. Instead, the educator engages in a process of "internal war" or "dispossession," where they strip away prior identities and received methodologies to confront the "disaster" at the limits of reason [S.42–S.43].

*   **Summary of Pedagogical Intent:**
    *   Education, in a context of crisis, is defined not by the transmission of established knowledge, but by a "displacement of horizons." It is an urgent, anxious task where teacher and student together encounter the "real" through a passage in thought [S.40].

Lesson 3: Pedagogies of Disaster - Presentation10 slides
Pedagogies of Disaster
  • Examining education in crisis contexts.
  • Navigating the intersection of pedagogical practice and disaster.
  • Challenging institutional constraints on academic discourse.

This text is an opening address by Vincent W.J. van Gerven Oei for the “Pedagogies of Disaster” conference (p. 29). The key points are summarized below:

The speaker explains that the conference is subject to contractual constraints imposed by its primary sponsor, Soros, which prohibit activities defined as “lobbying” (p. 29). This restriction specifically forbids “Calls to Action,” such as encouraging the public to influence legislation (p. 29). Consequently, the speaker expresses a cautious irony regarding his own idealistic appeal for an education system based on equality and open access, noting that the conference, by definition, must not function as a formal call to action (p. 29).

Using his prior experience teaching art students in Istanbul, the speaker illustrates the latent power of pedagogical work (p. 34). He observed that student art projects created months earlier took on an intensely political dimension once the students became involved in subsequent state-repressed protests (p. 34). 

The speaker concludes with a theoretical distinction: although the conference cannot be an explicit call to action in the present, he hopes it will have functioned as one in the future, acting as a catalyst for political and pedagogical engagement (p. 34). He concludes by acknowledging the various sponsors and colleagues who made the event possible (p. 34).

This text explores the concept of a "pedagogy of disaster," reflecting on the role of the educator when traditional institutional structures and political certainties collapse.

The key points are:

1. The Crisis of the University: Christopher Fynsk draws upon Jean-François Lyotard’s 1978 essay, "Endurance and the Profession" [S.38], to examine what it means to teach philosophy when the "desiccating forces" of modern bureaucracy and capitalism strip the educator of institutional authority and clear teleological goals [S.38-39].

2. Dispossession and Pedagogical Anguish: Confronted with the lack of rules and societal expectations, the teacher experiences a radical dispossession. This creates an state of "anguish"—a sovereign pressure to "go there" without knowing specifically where "there" is [S.39]. This pedagogical stance involves abandoning the role of *maître à penser* (a leader of thought) in favor of an obscure, impelled process of inquiry [S.40-41].

3. Intellectual Practice as Transformation: Fynsk argues that true teaching is not about changing the world in a visible, political sense, but about shifting one's own "worldhood" and "displacement of horizons" [S.41]. By engaging in an ascetic, rigorous process of research that refuses to rely on received commentary, the teacher invites a change in the state of their intellectual being [S.40].

4. The Humanities and Political Responsibility: The author connects these reflections to his own work, *The Claim of Language*. He suggests that when institutional foundations fail, the humanities must recover their status as fundamental research. This is not for the sake of pragmatic institutional justification, but as a way to engage with the question of the "human" [S.43].

5. Setting Philosophy Beside Itself: Ultimately, the text argues that in the context of institutional and political disaster, the only way forward is to perform a kind of "deculturation" [S.42]. By setting philosophy "beside itself," the educator seeks an unfinished, non-authoritative engagement that challenges both the institutions of the mind and the identity of the thinker [S.41-42].

This text explores the historical and philosophical rivalry between theater and philosophy, the evolution of the concept of the "school," and the contemporary state of education in the context of neoliberalism.

The Rivalry Between Philosophy and Theater
The author draws on Plato’s work to argue that philosophy defines its identity by excluding theater. While theater involves an intervention into the appearances of the city-state, Plato viewed it as a "degraded imitation" that threatened his vision of a just, orderly city. Philosophy attempted to resolve this conflict by establishing the school as a tool to impose order and educate citizens, effectively replacing the disruptive nature of theater with a controlled, institutional environment [S.68].

The Shift from City-State to Society
Historically, the goal of education was to "school to set the city-state right." Under modernity, this mission shifted toward "schooling to better society." However, as this project faces failure in the twenty-first century, the author proposes two necessary tasks for a new epoch: de-socializing the school—removing it from its current subservience to societal functions—and resituating it within an "action-zone" of political practice [S.69].

Critique of Institutional Schooling
The text analyzes Ivan Illich’s argument that institutional schools act as bureaucratic entities that standardize assessment to create social hierarchies and artificial needs. While Illich’s critique of the institutionalization of learning is noted, the author observes that his call for "deschooling" has been co-opted by neoliberal policies. Neoliberal agendas have successfully privatized public education, replacing the teacher with digital platforms and reducing education to "life-long learning" that caters to corporate needs [S.70-71].

The Crisis of Contemporary Education
The emergence of Massive Online Open Courses (MOOCs) and platforms like Khan Academy represents a significant shift in educational ideology. Programs like Khan Academy promote an individualistic approach that prizes "rhythm" and efficiency over collective experiences. By inverting the classroom model—where instruction happens at home and homework at school—these systems erode the role of the teacher as a rival master to capital and remove the fundamental post-revolutionary commitment to equality in learning [S.77].

The Need for a New Direction
Ultimately, the author asserts that the contemporary privatization of schools is a more dangerous, comprehensive version of the "deschooling" critique that Illich once envisioned. To be progressive in the current educational climate, one must resist these privatizing movements while simultaneously recognizing that the bureaucratic structures of the welfare state inadvertently laid the groundwork for this commodification [S.77].

The provided text discusses the intersection of technology, academic structures, and the ethical function of the human imagination. The following summary outlines the core arguments:

**The Crisis of the University in Technics**
The author critiques the modern university system, which increasingly relies on Learning Management Systems and automated software. This shift risks reducing human intellectual engagement to a "user" experience governed by surveillance and global capitalism. The academic environment is depicted as shifting from a site of intellectual freedom to one of technological enclosure, where student activity is tracked and reduced to digital data.

**The Role of Poetic Scope and Style**
Drawing from the prose of Wallace Stevens and the ethical philosophy of Emmanuel Levinas, the text argues that poetic style provides a means to resist the monotony of technics. Stevens’s concepts of "scope"—defined as an adaptive aperture for imaginative force—and "poetic style" are presented as tools for individuals to reclaim their humanity within a mechanized world. The author posits that the human task is to cultivate an ethical imagination that reaches toward a "radical exteriority," effectively bypassing the narrow, functional logic of modern technocratic institutions.

**The Ethics of the "Few" and Potentiality**
Stevens differentiates between the "technician" (a neutered, functional thinker) and the "all-round" thinker. The text explores the possibility of a transition represented by "the few"—individuals who maintain a benign and rapacious engagement with reality. By treating the university as a space in "ruins," the author suggests that we must move beyond factitious institutional history to consider the "usable past" and generate new forms of life. The ultimate ethical imperative is to maintain the vitality of imagination against the "militarization of culture" and the instrumentalization of thought, ensuring that science and technology remain expressions of a fundamentally human, poetic inquiry.

This text, likely drawn from the pages provided (S.128-S.134), explores the philosophical necessity of rethinking the nature of "thinking" itself, particularly in the context of the modern university's perceived crisis. 

Here are the key points:

Distinguishing Thinking from Thought
The author argues that we must move beyond viewing thinking as a mere theory or an institutional commodity (a "Denkanstalt"). Instead, thinking must be understood as an active, ongoing "a-way" or path ($going\ toward$) that does not seek a defined goal or "firmament." Following Heidegger and Deleuze, the text emphasizes that thinking should "engender thinking in thought" rather than simply creating more theory (p. 128, 130).

The Concept of the Disaster
Central to the work is Maurice Blanchot’s concept of the "disaster." Contrary to common usage, the disaster here does not refer to a catastrophe, but rather exists etymologically as a "break with the star" or a lack of totality (p. 131). It is an experience of the "outside" that cannot be definitively captured or solidified into knowledge. The disaster represents a "limit-experience" that escapes the conventional binary of true or false (p. 131-132).

Rethinking the University
The author rejects the idea of dwelling in the "ruins of the university" (a critique of Bill Readings). Rather than looking for a future-oriented solution or another institutional form, the text suggests that we must encounter the "elsewhere." This involves a commitment to questioning that refuses to settle into a "beyond" or a traditional end-point (p. 128, 132).

The Call to Remain Underway
Ultimately, the text calls for a form of reflection that remains "attentive." True thinking is not an act of reaction or a means to an end, but a "most thought-provoking" state of being that remains to be thought. The author posits that we have "acted too much and thought too little," and thus advocates for a posture of persistent questioning that keeps us "underway" without arriving at a final, closed destination (p. 129-130).

This section explores the "rhetoric of disaster" as a tool for managing contemporary anxieties, particularly concerning the future of the humanities and the university.

**Key Arguments**

1. The Rhetoric of Disaster: The author argues that talk of the "end of the university" and the "end of the humanities" functions as a form of "disaster-speak." Rather than being an objective analysis of events, this rhetoric serves to manage uncertainty and dictate survival strategies through the anticipation of a coming catastrophe (p. 148).

2. Strategy of Survival: To deflate the sense of impending doom, the author proposes treating the disaster as an event that has already occurred. By positioning the humanities within a "post-apocalyptic" framework, one can detach from the paralyzing affective machinery of constant crisis and focus instead on "survival" — defined by Blanchot's notion of living beyond or over an event (p. 149).

3. The Zombie Narrative as Model: The author uses the zombie apocalypse as an analytical model for a "survival manual." Zombie narratives often enforce a single, brutal protocol: not-becoming-zombie. This necessitates the prioritization of practical, essentialist values over the perceived "soft" values of the humanities (p. 150-151).

4. Education as a Source of Instability: The text identifies a historical tension where education is viewed by power structures as a dangerous, "contagious" force that disrupts social order. By fostering aspirations that society cannot meet, the humanities are cast as a threat to the "management" of the population, leading to calls for austerity and the rationing of educational opportunities (p. 151).

5. Redefining Normality: The author notes that disasters are no longer "post-normal" but have become the new "Normal." In this "risk society," the rhetoric of disaster is used to interpellate subjects under the constant pressure of a state of exception, shifting the focus from mourning past events to managing uncertain futures (p. 152-153).

This text, titled *A Passion for Yes* by Vincent W.J. van Gerven Oei, explores the philosophical and linguistic complexities of the word "yes" and its relationship to truth, affirmation, and technology.

**Key Themes and Arguments**

The Transcendental Ambiguity of "Yes"
The author argues that there is a significant "abyss" between the act of saying "yes" and the concept of affirmation (pp. 176-177). Drawing on Jacques Derrida, the text suggests that "yes" acts as a transcendental condition of speech, yet it is inherently unstable and untranslatable. It functions as a response to a call or a hearing of the other, but it is always subject to distortion—modeled by the metaphor of the telephone—where repetition can lead to mechanical parody rather than genuine affirmation (pp. 176-177).

Technological Mediation and Trauma
The text shifts the "telephonic" nature of "yes" to the context of television and digital media. The author, citing Avital Ronell, suggests that televised communication is rooted in "pure testimony" and trauma (p. 178). Because television disrupts the normal channels of experience and transmission, the pedagogical moment—the point where a student "gets it"—is constantly at risk of being suspended or repressed within this traumatic digital environment (p. 178).

"Coming Out" as a Parrhesiastic Act
The author examines "coming out" (e.g., "yes, I am gay") as a potential limit case of Michel Foucault’s *parrhēsia* (fearless speech). While this speech act serves as an essential part of the "care of the self," it is philosophically paradoxical: it affirms a truth that incurs danger to the speaker and simultaneously destabilizes the speaker's assumed authority to speak (pp. 182-183).

Poetry and the Future
Using a reading of Wallace Stevens’s poem *The Well Dressed Man with a Beard*, the author connects the act of saying "yes" to the creation of a "future world" (p. 184). Despite the technological obstacles and the inherent gap between speech and affirmation, the text proposes a "pedagogical yes"—a commitment to speech that sustains itself without relying on the immediate, externalized need for confirmation (p. 184).

The essential tension of this linguistic and philosophical inquiry can be summarized by the persistent, un-closable gap between the utterance and the internal realization:

$Yes \neq Affirmation$

The provided text explores the tension between traditional multicultural education in museums and the philosophical ethics of hospitality. Below is a concise summary of the key arguments:

**The Critique of Multiculturalism and Cosmopolitanism**
The author critiques the current pedagogical approach in museums, which relies on multiculturalism and Kantian cosmopolitan ideals of social inclusion. This model seeks to "fashion the global citizen" through a politics of recognition, where the "Stranger" is either invited in as a target audience or assimilated into legalistic categories. The text argues that this process of "passport cosmopolitanism" eliminates personal risk and maintains a sovereign sense of identity, ultimately failing to engage with the true "other."

**Hospitality vs. Social Inclusion**
Drawing on T.S. Eliot, Jacques Derrida, and Emmanuel Levinas, the author contrasts the inclusion of the stranger as a social instrument with the ethical act of "welcoming the stranger." True hospitality is defined as:
- A risk that disrupts the host's sense of home and sovereign identity.
- An asymmetrical relation that refuses the "aggregations of provisional selves" found in current community-based programming (p. 195).
- A departure from the "logic of the Same," where the stranger is no longer viewed as an object to be studied or a citizen to be formed (p. 194).

**The Re-envisioning of Museum Pedagogy**
The text proposes a tectonic shift in how museums function educationally:
- Moving away from "object-based" or "community-based" curation that seeks to represent culture or create consensus. 
- Welcoming the child as a "stranger" at the threshold who possesses the capacity to ask fundamental questions (p. 194).
- Embracing the "infinite obligation" to the other, using the example of Abraham in Genesis, whose hospitality is devoid of reciprocity, contractual pacts, or the mastery of a sovereign host (p. 193).

**Conclusion**
The author concludes that for museum education to be truly transformative, it must move beyond the "impasse" of multiculturalism. By treating hospitality not as a right to be legislated, but as an inherent risk to one's own subjectivity, museums can move from a mode of exclusionary tolerance toward a genuine, face-to-face encounter with alterity (p. 197).

The project "Occupy Baghdad" by Iraqi-born artist Urok Shirhan explores the concept of "non-physical occupation," arguing that the image and idea of Iraq have been occupied by global cultural and artistic production (p. 216, 232).

Key points of the project include:

The Mediatized War
Shirhan posits that the Iraq War is being waged on two fronts: the physical war on the ground and a "mediatized" war of representation. Because more people participate in the consumption and production of images regarding the war than fight in it, Baghdad has become a "public good" accessible to global audiences, effectively detaching it from its physical borders and the privacy of its inhabitants (p. 225, 237).

The Archive and Artistic Complicity
The "Occupy Baghdad Archive" is a critical initiative that gathers artworks created outside of Iraq concerning the war. These works are brought together to examine the ethical implications of representation, questioning whether such art can be complicit in war crimes and whether artists share responsibility for the occupation (p. 219, 235).

The "Membership of the Occupation of Iraq"
To address this complicity, Shirhan created a "Membership of the Occupation of Iraq" document. This framework serves as a tool for individuals to acknowledge their role in the mediatized reality of the war. By checking conditions such as consuming information about the invasion or benefiting from industries—like oil—that supported the geopolitical context of the war, participants are led to confront their lack of neutrality. The ultimate goal of the project is to create an explicit awareness of this involvement so that participants may take responsibility for ending their "membership" in this collective, mediatized occupation (p. 226, 227, 238, 239).

Video Research
The project originated when Shirhan discovered that footage filmed by her father in Baghdad in 2003 bore a haunting, near-identical resemblance to a 2002 video work by American artist Paul Chan. This "coincidence" served as the catalyst for her research into how artistic production replicates and sustains specific narratives of conflict (p. 217, 233).

This text, written by Jonas Staal, explores the relationship between art, politics, and the state through the lens of "democratism"—a manipulated, repressive version of democracy.

**The Guantánamo Interrogation as a Metaphor**
Staal analyzes leaked interrogation logs from Guantánamo, where detainees were subjected to "intellectual mass torture" using Western icons of liberation—such as pop music (Christina Aguilera, Metallica), fast food, and sexually suggestive imagery. The author highlights the irony of a detainee, al-Khatani, choosing to talk about dinosaurs as a radical attempt to escape the oppressive, simulated reality of his captors. Staal argues that this torture is not an anomaly but a distorted reflection of the daily bombardment of symbols that define modern "liberal democracy."

**Democracy vs. Democratism**
Using a historical anecdote about Lenin in Japan, Staal distinguishes between "democracy" and "democratism." He suggests that the word "democracy" has been co-opted to serve capitalist interests, which he refers to as "democratism." This ideology masks state violence, state secrecy (such as "terrorist lists"), and anti-democratic operations behind a rhetoric of freedom and enlightenment.

**The Role of Art After Democratism**
Staal proposes that art should serve as a weapon to reclaim emancipatory politics. He advocates for "art after democratism," which requires artists to:
- Rearticulate the political context in which they operate.
- Recognize art as a political force rather than a passive byproduct of political systems.
- Use the "juridical exceptional role of art" to create spaces that bypass state-imposed constraints, as demonstrated by his project, the "New World Summit."

**New World Summit**
The summit functions as a nomadic, alternative parliament for political organizations that have been sidelined or labeled as "terrorists" by state committees, such as the EU's "Clearing House." By giving these groups a platform, the project aims to expose the "democratic deficit" of current governance and move toward a "fundamental democracy" that exists apart from the state. Staal concludes that the ultimate goal of progressive, radical art is to liberate democracy from the state itself.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: In his opening address, why does Vincent W.J. van Gerven Oei state that 'this conference cannot be a call to action'?
options: Because the conference organizers explicitly decided to avoid political discourse.
Because of the funding conditions imposed by a sponsor to avoid lobbying efforts.
Because the conference participants requested a purely theoretical environment.
Because the Albanian government legally prohibited institutional advocacy.
questions.explanation: The author explains he signed a funding contract with Soros that prohibited 'lobbying,' which was defined within the document as including 'a Call to Action' (encouraging the public to contact legislators).

questions.question: What lesson did van Gerven Oei draw from his students' artistic work created before and during the Turkish protests?
options: Artistic training is essential for effective street-level political activism.
Academic institutions are often too slow to recognize the value of student political engagement.
Pedagogical work that appears apolitical may gain political significance in future contexts.
Students should be encouraged to prioritize direct protest over academic exhibition.
questions.explanation: The author notes that work he considered purely pedagogical/artistic suddenly became 'intensely political' in hindsight due to changing circumstances, demonstrating that work today can become a 'call to action' at a future point.

questions.question: How does Christopher Fynsk characterize the 'pedagogy of disaster' exemplified by Lyotard at Vincennes?
options: A state of radical autonomy where the teacher is stripped of institutional authority.
A curriculum focused exclusively on emergency response and survival skills.
A structured hierarchy designed to protect the humanities from collapse.
A strategic effort to rebuild university structures through modern rhetoric.
questions.explanation: Fynsk describes the situation at Vincennes as a 'pedagogy of disaster' because it occurred in a vacuum of institutional support, leading to the 'disappropriation' of the professor's traditional, sanctioned authority.

questions.question: According to the excerpts, what is the 'sovereign pressure' felt by a teacher in the type of pedagogical situation Lyotard describes?
options: The need to impress colleagues with complex philosophical arguments.
The obligation to maintain order in an institutionally unprotected, 'stuttering' environment.
The fear of being replaced by more qualified, younger thinkers.
The necessity to guide students toward a specific, pre-determined ideological goal.
questions.explanation: Lyotard describes a sense of 'anguish'—not tied to professional stage fright, but to a 'sovereign pressure' to present one's work without the protection of prescribed rules, forcing the teacher to act without knowing exactly where the process is leading.

questions.question: What prompted the students and faculty at Vincennes to study the rhetoric of their own strike?
options: A mandate from the French secondary school administration.
A desire to create a new curriculum for philosophy students.
A confrontation with militants who denounced the 'magisterial function' of teaching.
A requirement to secure funding from external cultural organizations.
questions.explanation: Lyotard describes how militants would burst in to denounce traditional teaching roles. By turning their study toward the rhetoric used by the strikers themselves, the faculty were able to demonstrate that their own work was not fundamentally different from the strikers' actions.

cards.front: What is the central theme of the 'Pedagogies of Disaster' conference described in the text?
cards.back: Thinking through the current predicament of pedagogical practices, specifically within the humanities and philosophy.

cards.front: What condition did Vincent W.J. van Gerven Oei agree to regarding funding for the conference?
cards.back: He agreed that the conference would not engage in 'lobbying,' defined as efforts to influence legislation or issuing a 'Call to Action.'

cards.front: How does Vincent W.J. van Gerven Oei define a 'valuable lesson about pedagogical practice' regarding political engagement?
cards.back: That which may not be a call to action at the present moment has the potential of becoming one at any future point in time.

cards.front: Christopher Fynsk cites a 1978 essay by Jean-François Lyotard to orient his reflection on pedagogy. What is the title of the essay?
In Lyotard’s account of teaching at Vincennes, what does he mean when he says the university is 'sans titres and sans condition(s)'?
cards.back: It refers to a lack of formal degrees, lack of institutional authorization, and the absence of prescribed rules protecting the teacher.

cards.front: According to Lyotard, what is the 'sovereign pressure' felt by a teacher in a situation of pedagogical disaster?
cards.back: An imbecilic imperative he phrases as 'il faut y aller' (one must go there), which creates anguish because the 'there' is undefined.

cards.front: How does Lyotard characterize the teacher's authority when stripped of institutional protection?
cards.back: The teacher is 'disappropriated' of pre-given or institutionally sanctioned authority and must face students without notes or evasions.

cards.front: What anecdote does Lyotard use to illustrate the absurdity of the pedagogical environment at Vincennes?
cards.back: Leftist commandos bursting in to denounce the 'magisterial function' and 'star-system,' only to find the class studying the rhetoric of the strikers themselves.

cards.front: What are some of the primary organizations that supported the 'Pedagogies of Disaster' conference?
cards.back: continent., Fondacioni Shoqëria e Hapur për Shqipërinë (SOROS), Punctum Books, and the University of Aberdeen (Centre for Modern Thought).

cards.front: What does Lyotard conclude about the teacher's state of mind when a class 'works out well' in a state of disaster?
cards.back: He expresses a paradox: if you make them understand what you didn't really understand yourself, 'it didn't really work out.'

slides.title: Pedagogies of Disaster
bullets: Examining education in crisis contexts.
Navigating the intersection of pedagogical practice and disaster.
Challenging institutional constraints on academic discourse.
Reimagining the university beyond its traditional roles.
slides.notes: This presentation explores how pedagogical practice functions or struggles within disaster contexts, drawing on collective insights from the conference.

slides.title: A Pedagogy on the Verge
bullets: Pedagogical authority under institutional destitution.
Thinking as an act of radical freedom.
The university as an 'in-ruins' site of inquiry.
Moving beyond inherited definitions of pedagogical roles.
slides.notes: Christopher Fynsk examines how Lyotard’s concept of endurance informs a 'pedagogy of disaster' where traditional authority is dispossessed.

slides.title: Desocializing the School
bullets: Identifying the rivalry between theater and philosophy.
The failure of modern education to 'better society'.
Removing the school from society to create an 'action-zone'.
Moving beyond Platonic and modern educational models.
slides.notes: Oliver Feltham argues for de-socializing schooling to overcome the failures of modern institutions, positioning the school as a new action-zone.

slides.title: Sandy Hook University
bullets: The shift toward technics and surveillance in academia.
Imagination as a prize within technological order.
Reclaiming access and care in the pedagogical ambit.
The poetry of language creating passages for thinking.
slides.notes: Adam Staley Groves critiques the intersection of technology and imagination, urging a reclamation of space for thinking within the university.

slides.title: A Call for Thinking (the Disaster)
bullets: Thinking as a process that cannot be institutionally contained.
Revisiting the Heideggerian call to 'stay with the question'.
The university as a site to rethink thought itself.
Thinking the disaster as an original act.
slides.notes: Julia Hölzl argues for an 'elsewhere' of thinking, resisting institutional abstractions to re-engage the fundamental, precarious act of thought.

slides.title: The Rhetoric of Disaster
bullets: Critiquing 'disaster speak' in humanities and academia.
Developing survival protocols for the end of the humanities.
Taking disaster as a past-tense, lived event.
Using zombie narratives to test survival in the post-apocalyptic.
slides.notes: John Van Houdt proposes strategies for 'living beyond' the rhetoric of disaster, transforming the sense of doom into productive modes of survival.

slides.title: A Passion for Yes
bullets: The abyss between saying 'yes' and true affirmation.
The untranslatable, traumatic dimension of affirmation.
Language as an act of responsibility and conditionality.
The 'telephonic' nature of affirmation and distortion.
slides.notes: Vincent van Gerven Oei explores the meaning of 'yes' as an act of affirmation that consistently contains a trace of irresponsibility and distance.

slides.title: Welcoming the Stranger
bullets: Diffrentiating social inclusion from true hospitality.
The uncanny dispossession of identity through the stranger.
Challenging the commerce of the modern 'we'.
Hospitality as an ethical imperative outside the polis.
slides.notes: Edith Doron analyzes the encounter with the 'stranger' as a disruption of sovereign identity and a move toward exilic education.

slides.title: Occupy Baghdad
bullets: The non-physical occupation of images and ideas.
Artistic research into the mediatized war on Iraq.
Critiquing exclusive rights to cultural representation.
The dual nature of wars: physical and representational.
slides.notes: Urok Shirhan investigates how the 'occupation of images' parallels military conflict, challenging who has the right to represent a nation.

slides.title: Art After Democratism
bullets: Interrogating the mass intellectual torture of liberal democracy.
Symbols of liberation as tools of oppression.
Differentiating the language of emancipation from repression.
Reframing democratic education in the shadow of disaster.
slides.notes: Jonas Staal examines how state-sanctioned culture can function as intellectual torture, necessitating a new language to reclaim true democratic potential.
